| Learning styles matching | assumed large | Tailoring instruction to a student’s “visual” vs. “auditory” style substantially improves learning | Wide belief, vanishing evidence |
| Mozart effect | d large short-term | Listening to classical music briefly substantially raises spatial reasoning; early-childhood exposure permanently raises IQ | Rauscher, Shaw & Ky [@rauscher1993] ; stronger claims in popularization |
| Nun Study — early-life writing predicts Alzheimer’s | very strong | Autobiographical complexity at age 22 substantially predicts dementia risk 60 years later | Snowdon et al. |
:::

If this literature had been correct, closing achievement gaps would require only a brief psychological intervention, expertise would be a function of practice time alone, and IQ would be trainable. The actual state of evidence after bias correction is in Appendix A: growth mindset produces *d* = 0.02 in the highest-quality studies; brain training has zero far transfer; learning-styles matching has no reliable empirical support; deliberate practice explains 4% of variance in education and 1% among elite performers.

### C.7   Nutritional and lifestyle epidemiology {#c-nutri}

Schoenfeld and Ioannidis [@schoenfeld2013] examined 50 common cookbook ingredients and found that 40 of the 50 had been associated with cancer risk in published studies, with an average published relative risk of 2.20 across the set. Meta-analyses collapsed these associations to RR ≈ 0.96 --- a 23-fold inflation factor from single studies. Taken at published face value, the last forty years of nutritional epidemiology committed the reader to something like the following.

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 34. C.7 Nutritional and lifestyle epidemiology**

| Claim (at original published magnitude) | Implication if true | Source |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Coffee → cancer (varied specific sites) | Multiple daily exposures substantially change your oncologic risk | Pre-2000s epidemiology; since largely reversed |
| Eggs → cardiovascular death | Daily eggs substantially raise CVD mortality | Pre-RCT era; reversed |
| Moderate alcohol protective for CV mortality (J-curve) | One to two drinks daily substantially lowers all-cause mortality vs. abstention | Decades of observational; contradicted by MR [@holmes2014] |
| Vitamin E, β-carotene, selenium → cancer prevention | Inexpensive supplements substantially reduce cancer and CV mortality | Observational; null/harmful in RCTs (SELECT, CARET, ATBC) |
| HDL cholesterol protects against CVD | Pharmacologic HDL elevation should substantially reduce CV events | MR and RCTs (torcetrapib etc.) show non-causal |
| HRT protects against CVD | Long-term hormone therapy meaningfully reduces heart attacks | WHI [@investigators2002] reversed this |
| Red meat → cancer | Normal Western consumption substantially raises cancer risk | Associations much weaker after bias correction |
| Low-fat diet → CV protection | A population-level shift in macronutrients substantially reduces CVD | Women’s Health Initiative dietary trial: null |
:::

If the observational nutrition literature had been correct, diet would be the dominant lever for population health, supplements would have transformed longevity by 1990, and hormone replacement would have halved cardiovascular disease in postmenopausal women. Randomized trials have systematically reversed the largest of these claims.

### C.8   Economics, nudges, and campaign persuasion {#c-econ}

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 35. C.8 Economics, nudges, and campaign persuasion**

| Finding | Published effect | Implication if true | Source |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Academic nudge studies (social comparison, defaults, framing) | 8.7 percentage points average | Low-cost behavioral interventions produce substantial real-world changes when deployed at population scale | DellaVigna & Linos [@dellavigna2022] — academic sample |
| Microcredit as poverty reduction | Substantial positive effects | Small loans are a general-purpose anti-poverty instrument at population scale | 1990s observational and small-sample reports |
| Door-to-door canvassing on vote choice | Several pp per contact in early papers | A small number of well-targeted doorstep conversations could decide a national election | Pre-Kalla-Broockman literature |
| Contact hypothesis on prejudice | d large in early work | Brief positive cross-group contact substantially reduces prejudice long-term | Paluck & Green lit reviews reveal more mixed evidence |
| LaCour canvassing on gay marriage attitudes | d very large, durable | A single 20-minute conversation permanently shifts political attitudes on polarized issues | LaCour & Green [@lacour2014] — retracted 2015 for fabricated data |
| Financial literacy education → behavior | substantial in early work | Classroom financial education substantially improves real-world financial decisions | Fernandes, Lynch & Netemeyer [@fernandes2014] collapse this to r ≈ 0.04 |
| CEO effects on firm performance | reported large | Hiring the right CEO substantially determines shareholder returns | Bertrand & Schoar find small effects; most variance is firm and industry |
| Hurricane-name matching on charitable giving | d reported large | Sharing a first letter with a hurricane’s name substantially raises your probability of donating | Chandler et al. [@chandler2008] |
| Name-letter effect (Dennis → dentist) | substantial | People are meaningfully more likely to choose careers and spouses sharing letters with their own name | Pelham et al. [@pelham2002] series |
:::

If this literature were real, nudges would scale, campaigns would decide elections at small budgets, canvassing would resolve culture-war disputes, and the career you chose was substantially determined by your own first initial. The actual scale effects (1.4 pp for nudges at government scale), the actual canvassing effects on vote choice (indistinguishable from zero in Kalla & Broockman [@kalla2018]), and the actual robustness of the name-letter literature to confounders are documented in Appendix A.

### C.9   Parapsychology --- the limit case {#c-psi}

The strongest test of whether the standard methodological toolkit of psychology can manufacture false positives at publication-quality effect sizes is whether it can produce significant evidence for effects known in advance to be impossible. It can.

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 36. C.9 Parapsychology — the limit case**

| Finding | Published effect | Implication if true | Source |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Precognition of future stimuli | 9 experiments, p < 0.05 in 8 | The effect of a future event can be detected in present behavior; time-reversed causation in human cognition | Bem 2011 JPSP |
| Telepathic ganzfeld hit rates | ~32% vs. 25% chance | Mind-to-mind information transfer operates at a small but reliable rate | Honorton and meta-analyses |
| Global Consciousness Project — RNG deviations during major events | p small across events | Collective human attention substantially influences physical random-number generators | PEAR lab and successors |
:::

Bem [@bem2011] was published in the field's leading journal using its standard methods. The study is methodologically indistinguishable from hundreds of uncontested publications. It is included here because it establishes the upper bound on what the standard toolkit can produce: a result with *p* \< 0.05 for an effect that is nomologically impossible. If the methods that produced Bem also produced the rest of the social priming literature, the results are not differentially credible.

### C.10   Animal models and preclinical translation {#c-preclin}

The preclinical animal literature reports substantial therapeutic effects in roughly every disease model in which it is tested. If taken at published face value, thousands of interventions should work in humans. Approximately 5% actually do [@ineichen2024]. Several representative classes:

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 37. C.10 Animal models and preclinical translation**

| Claim (at preclinical published magnitude) | Implication if true | Source |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Hundreds of neuroprotective agents for stroke | Dozens of drugs should prevent stroke damage in humans | ALL failed in human trials until tPA |
| Many anti-amyloid agents for Alzheimer’s | Reducing amyloid plaque in mice should translate to substantial cognitive benefit in humans | Decades of failures before marginal anti-amyloid antibody effects |
| Cancer biology reproducibility | Landmark oncology findings should reliably replicate across labs | Errington et al. [@errington2021] : effects 85% smaller; 40% replication rate |
:::

Publication bias in preclinical stroke inflates effects by \~31% [@sena2010] and small-sample bias compounds this. If the full preclinical literature had been correct, modern medicine would already have cured the major neurodegenerative and cerebrovascular diseases, rather than confronting them.

### C.11   What taking the literature at face value commits you to {#c-sum}

Collecting the appendix into a single inventory of the world implied by the published literature before replication arrived:

-   Every ambient environmental cue --- cup temperature, object weight, color, smell, background image, posture --- substantially reshapes your cognition and judgment within minutes.
-   Self-control is a quantifiable liquid battery that discharges across the day and recharges on sugar, with measurable consequences for judicial, professional, and moral decisions.
-   Standardized testing is largely a priming experiment, and much of the racial and gender achievement gap is manipulable in a half-hour session.
-   Specific individual genetic variants substantially determine depression, aggression, personality, cognition, and parenting.
-   A 30-minute MRI scan reveals deep individual differences at correlations of *r* \> 0.7.
-   Expertise is overwhelmingly practice; talent is small; IQ is trainable; brief classroom interventions close achievement gaps.
-   Diet is the dominant driver of chronic disease; supplements substantially extend life; hormone replacement protects the heart.
-   Behavioral nudges solve policy at population scale; canvassing decides elections; microcredit ends poverty; financial literacy education fixes household finance.
-   The careers you choose and the people you marry are non-trivially determined by letter-match heuristics with your own name.
-   Precognition and telepathy are measurable phenomena at small but reliable effect sizes, and collective consciousness perturbs physical hardware.
-   Almost every preclinical therapeutic finding translates to human benefit.

None of these propositions, taken at the magnitudes the original literature claimed, survived adequate-powered replication. Most survived partially, with effect sizes one-half to one-tenth of the published values and heterogeneity sufficient to prevent confident application. Several are genuine methodological artifacts of a pre-registration, pre-replication era and have vanished entirely under scrutiny.

The point of Appendix C is not that the investigators who produced these findings were uniformly mistaken or dishonest. Most were neither. The point is that the standard methodological toolkit, operating on small samples, with undisclosed analytic flexibility, in the presence of strong publication filters, was capable of producing a literature that described a world that does not exist. That literature was taken seriously for decades. It shaped clinical practice, educational policy, public health messaging, and the popular understanding of human nature. The gap between the world Appendix C describes and the world Appendices A and B describe is the scale of the correction still in progress.

*The upshot of Appendix C: the literature we retired was a literature describing a universe whose rules differ from ours. We do not live in a world where holding a warm cup rewires trust by a third of a standard deviation. We lived for two decades in one that acted like we did.*

## Appendix D --- Replication pairs: original vs. large-N replication

The current replication-pair build contains 1,736 usable original/replication endpoint pairs. Across those rows, 80% of effects shrink and 40% drop below the p \< .05 boundary at the replication sample size.

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 38. Appendix D — Replication pairs: original vs. large-N replication**

| # | Study | N orig | d orig | N repl | d repl | Replication |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | Strack pen-in-teeth | 92 | 0.82 | 1,894 | 0.03 | RRR Wagenmakers 2016 |
| 2 | Baumeister radishes/ego depletion | 67 | 1.76 | 2,141 | 0.04 | RRR Hagger 2016 |
| 3 | Schooler verbal overshadowing (RRR1) | 90 | 0.80 | 2,319 | 0.03 | RRR Alogna 2014 |
| 4 | Schooler verbal overshadowing (RRR2) | 90 | 0.80 | 3,026 | 0.16 | RRR Alogna 2014 v2 |
| 5 | Dijksterhuis professor priming | 80 | 0.51 | 4,493 | 0.01 | RRR O’Donnell 2018 |
| 6 | Mazar/Amir/Ariely moral reminders | 229 | 0.48 | 5,786 | 0.02 | RRR Verschuere 2018 |
| 7 | Srull-Wyer hostility priming | 96 | 0.35 | 7,300 | 0.06 | RRR McCarthy 2018 |
| 8 | Rand et al intuitive cooperation | 343 | 0.40 | 3,596 | 0.01 | RRR Bouwmeester 2017 |
| 9 | Finkel commitment/forgiveness | 98 | 0.40 | 5,070 | 0.28 | RRR Cheung 2016 |
| 10 | Bargh elderly priming | 30 | 0.70 | 120 | 0.02 | Doyen 2012 |
| 11 | Carney power pose | 42 | 0.65 | 200 | 0.02 | Ranehill 2015 |
| 12 | Bem precognition | 100 | 0.22 | 3,289 | 0.01 | Galak 2012 |
| 13 | Anchoring 1 (Tversky 1974) | 152 | 0.88 | 6,344 | 0.70 | Many Labs 1 |
| 14 | Retrospective gambler’s fallacy | 105 | 0.80 | 6,344 | 0.50 | Many Labs 1 |
| 15 | Anchoring 2 | 152 | 0.72 | 6,344 | 0.54 | Many Labs 1 |
| 16 | Anchoring 3 | 152 | 0.71 | 6,344 | 0.54 | Many Labs 1 |
| 17 | Gain/loss framing (Tversky 1981) | 122 | 0.70 | 6,344 | 0.60 | Many Labs 1 |
| 18 | Gambler’s fallacy (Oppenheimer 2009) | 59 | 0.67 | 6,344 | 0.70 | Many Labs 1 |
| 19 | Anchoring 4 | 152 | 0.65 | 6,344 | 0.48 | Many Labs 1 |
| 20 | Allowed/forbidden asymmetry | 122 | 0.60 | 6,344 | 0.35 | Many Labs 1 |
| 21 | Sunk cost (Oppenheimer 2009) | 211 | 0.53 | 6,344 | 0.46 | Many Labs 1 |
| 22 | Quote attribution (anchoring) | 89 | 0.48 | 6,344 | 0.30 | Many Labs 1 |
| 23 | Imagined contact (Husnu 2010) | 144 | 0.38 | 6,344 | 0.01 | Many Labs 1 |
| 24 | Flag priming (Carter 2011) | 235 | 0.30 | 6,344 | 0.01 | Many Labs 1 |
| 25 | Currency priming (Caruso 2013) | 148 | 0.30 | 6,344 | 0.01 | Many Labs 1 |
| 26 | Hauser 2007 trolley (side-effect) | 2,646 | 2.50 | 6,842 | 1.35 | Many Labs 2 |
| 27 | Miyamoto 2002 correspondence bias | 58 | 1.75 | 7,197 | 1.82 | Many Labs 2 |
| 28 | Knobe 2003 side-effect intentionality | 78 | 1.45 | 7,982 | 1.75 | Many Labs 2 |
| 29 | Zhong-Liljenquist 2006 Macbeth | 27 | 1.02 | 7,001 | 0.00 | Many Labs 2 |
| 30 | Ross 1977 false consensus (supermarket) | 80 | 0.99 | 7,205 | 1.18 | Many Labs 2 |
| 31 | Tversky-Kahneman 1981 framing | 181 | 0.89 | 7,228 | 0.40 | Many Labs 2 |
| 32 | Bauer 2012 consumerism/trust | 77 | 0.87 | 6,608 | 0.12 | Many Labs 2 |
| 33 | Ross 1977 false consensus (traffic) | 80 | 0.80 | 7,827 | 0.95 | Many Labs 2 |
| 34 | Gray-Wegner 2009 moral typecasting | 69 | 0.80 | 8,002 | 0.95 | Many Labs 2 |
| 35 | Rottenstreich-Hsee 2001 affect/probability | 40 | 0.74 | 7,218 | -0.08 | Many Labs 2 |
| 36 | Inbar 2009 disgust/gay-kissing | 44 | 0.70 | 7,117 | 0.05 | Many Labs 2 |
| 37 | Hsee 1998 less-is-better | 83 | 0.69 | 7,646 | 0.78 | Many Labs 2 |

| 495 | Seaton 2010 (education) | 265,180 | 0.07 | 62,880 | 0.43 | SCORE main |
| 496 | Hiskey 2018 (political science) | 4,648 | 0.06 | 7,013 | 0.00 | SCORE main |
| 497 | Chen 2018 (sociology and criminology) | 3,617 | 0.05 | 8,227 | 0.02 | SCORE main |
| 498 | Echávarri 2016 (economics and finance) | 43,576 | 0.05 | 305,244 | 0.02 | SCORE main |
| 499 | Boehm 2015 (psychology and health) | 4,458 | 0.05 | 4,971 | 0.06 | SCORE main |
| 500 | Mclaren 2012 (political science) | 110,732 | 0.05 | 89,939 | 0.02 | SCORE main |
| 501 | Mclaren 2012 (political science) | 110,732 | 0.05 | 90,130 | 0.07 | SCORE main |
| 502 | Button 2017 (sociology and criminology) | 7,741 | 0.04 | 7,101 | 0.03 | SCORE main |
| 503 | Boehm 2015 (psychology and health) | 4,458 | 0.04 | 4,971 | 0.11 | SCORE main |
| 504 | Welzel 2012 (political science) | 73,175 | 0.04 | 60,452 | 0.11 | SCORE main |
| 505 | Welzel 2012 (political science) | 73,175 | 0.03 | 59,584 | 0.03 | SCORE main |
| 506 | Welzel 2012 (political science) | 73,175 | 0.03 | 59,584 | 0.09 | SCORE main |
| 507 | Roberts 2010 (education) | 12,012 | 0.03 | 13,281 | 0.08 | SCORE main |
| 508 | Thomas 2012 (sociology and criminology) | 160,274 | 0.02 | 110,747 | 0.03 | SCORE main |
| 509 | Teney 2016 (sociology and criminology) | 27 | 0.01 | 28 | 0.02 | SCORE main |
| 510 | Travers 2018 (education) | 7,977,660 | 0.00 | 49,196,731 | 0.00 | SCORE main |
| 511 | Nichols 2004 Phil Psych | 54 | 1.44 | 131 | 1.27 | Cova 2018 xphi |
| 512 | Liao 2014 BJA | 30 | 1.28 | 99 | 1.32 | Cova 2018 xphi |
| 513 | Buckwalter & Schaffer 2015 Nous | 264 | 1.24 | 442 | 0.90 | Cova 2018 xphi |
| 514 | Sarkissian 2011 M&L | 223 | 0.87 | 363 | 0.67 | Cova 2018 xphi |
| 515 | Sytsma & Machery 2010 Phil Stud | 295 | 0.87 | 180 | 0.52 | Cova 2018 xphi |
| 516 | Nichols 2006 JCC | 8 | 0.75 | 50 | 0.44 | Cova 2018 xphi |
| 517 | Hitchcock & Knobe 2009 J Phil | 3,422 | 0.74 | 200 | 0.63 | Cova 2018 xphi |
| 518 | Beebe & Buckwalter 2010 M&L | 749 | 0.74 | 514 | 0.66 | Cova 2018 xphi |
| 519 | Nahmias 2007 Midwest Phil | 632 | 0.70 | 183 | 0.69 | Cova 2018 xphi |
| 520 | Nadelhoffer 2004 JTPP | 44 | 0.69 | 47 | 0.83 | Cova 2018 xphi |
| 521 | Roxborough & Cumby 2009 Phil Psych | 118 | 0.69 | 204 | 0.20 | Cova 2018 xphi |
| 522 | Schaffer & Knobe 2012 Nous | 100 | 0.68 | 150 | 0.58 | Cova 2018 xphi |
| 523 | Machery 2004 Cognition | 72 | 0.61 | 181 | 0.00 | Cova 2018 xphi |
| 524 | Knobe 2004 Analysis | 77 | 0.61 | 127 | 1.27 | Cova 2018 xphi |
| 525 | Knobe & Burra 2006 JCC | 61 | 0.61 | 102 | 0.61 | Cova 2018 xphi |
| 526 | Knobe 2003a Analysis | 78 | 0.59 | 100 | 0.61 | Cova 2018 xphi |
| 527 | Beebe & Shea 2013 Episteme | 376 | 0.58 | 418 | 0.74 | Cova 2018 xphi |
| 528 | Kominsky 2015 Cognition | 60 | 0.57 | 200 | 0.58 | Cova 2018 xphi |
| 529 | Nahmias 2005 Phil Psych | 21 | 0.52 | 186 | 0.60 | Cova 2018 xphi |
| 530 | Alicke 2011 J Phil | 319 | 0.50 | 327 | 0.14 | Cova 2018 xphi |
| 531 | Knobe 2003b Phil Psych | 80 | 0.46 | 292 | 0.60 | Cova 2018 xphi |
| 532 | Nadelhoffer 2005 Phil Psych | 40 | 0.45 | 128 | 0.38 | Cova 2018 xphi |
| 533 | Paxton 2012 Cog Sci | 92 | 0.43 | 298 | 0.13 | Cova 2018 xphi |
| 534 | Livengood & Machery 2007 MidPhil | 95 | 0.42 | 272 | 0.57 | Cova 2018 xphi |
| 535 | Grau & Pury 2014 RPP | 162 | 0.41 | 387 | 0.20 | Cova 2018 xphi |
| 536 | May & Holton 2012 Phil Stud | 117 | 0.38 | 368 | 0.28 | Cova 2018 xphi |
| 537 | Nichols & Knobe 2007 Nous | 41 | 0.38 | 80 | 0.73 | Cova 2018 xphi |
| 538 | De Brigard 2010 Phil Psych | 72 | 0.36 | 87 | 0.27 | Cova 2018 xphi |
| 539 | Nahmias 2006 PPR | 86 | 0.32 | 182 | 0.12 | Cova 2018 xphi |
| 540 | Nadelhoffer & Feltz 2008 Neuroethics | 85 | 0.30 | 150 | 0.00 | Cova 2018 xphi |
| 541 | Björnsson 2015 Phil Psych | 248 | 0.30 | 456 | 0.26 | Cova 2018 xphi |
| 542 | Nadelhoffer 2006 Phil Explor | 126 | 0.25 | 259 | 0.41 | Cova 2018 xphi |
| 543 | McCann 2005 Phil Psych | 106 | 0.15 | 300 | 0.04 | Cova 2018 xphi |
| 544 | Bigot-misanthrope (Uhlmann) | 46 | 1.81 | 3,040 | 2.38 | Pipeline Project 2016 |
| 545 | Moral cliff (Zhu & Uhlmann) | 114 | 1.37 | 3,592 | 0.40 | Pipeline Project 2016 |
| 546 | Cold-hearted prosociality (Uhlmann) | 79 | 1.22 | 3,016 | 0.92 | Pipeline Project 2016 |
| 547 | Higher standards, charity (Srinivasan) | 265 | 0.93 | 2,888 | 0.88 | Pipeline Project 2016 |
| 548 | Intuitive economics (Uhlmann-Diermeier) | 226 | 0.85 | 3,192 | 1.12 | Pipeline Project 2016 |
| 549 | Moral inversion (Uhlmann) | 130 | 0.78 | 3,133 | 0.37 | Pipeline Project 2016 |
| 550 | Bad tipper (Uhlmann) | 79 | 0.64 | 3,706 | 0.63 | Pipeline Project 2016 |
| 551 | Burn in hell (Uhlmann-Diermeier) | 158 | 0.46 | 3,430 | 0.48 | Pipeline Project 2016 |
| 552 | Belief-act inconsistency (Uhlmann) | 192 | 0.38 | 3,708 | 0.33 | Pipeline Project 2016 |
| 553 | Higher standards, company (Srinivasan) | 265 | 0.35 | 2,888 | 1.04 | Pipeline Project 2016 |
| 554 | Nudges academic vs gov at scale | 30,000 | 0.20 | 24,000,000 | 0.03 | DellaVigna-Linos 2022 |
| 555 | Brain-behavior r (BWAS pre-2022) | 25 | 1.50 | 50,000 | 0.16 | Marek 2022 BWAS |
:::

### Aggregate replication-project summaries

Some coordinated replication projects released aggregate meta-analytic statistics as an addition to per-experiment data. These are reported below.

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 39. Aggregate replication-project summaries**

| Project | Experiments | Orig d (median) | Repl d (median) | Replication rate | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Reproducibility Project: Cancer Biology (Errington et al. 2021) | 50 experiments from 23 papers; 158 effects | 2.96 | 0.43 | 46% (39/97 positive effects on ≥3 of 5 criteria) | mean SMD 5.41 → 1.19; 85% effect-size shrinkage; animal experiments 12% replication, in vitro 54% |
| SCORE project (Tyner et al. 2026, Nature) | 274 claims from 164 papers across 6 disciplines; 282 plotted here from the analyst_data.RData master file | 0.49 | 0.25 | 55.1% overall (Tyner 2026); ≈50% shrinkage in median d | OSF repo g5sny/bzfgy; per-claim partial correlations converted to Cohen’s d via standard formula |
| Experimental philosophy replicability (Cova et al. 2021) | 40 replications of published x-phi studies; 33 with complete pairs | — | — | 70% (24/34 with sufficient power) | mean original r ≈ 0.39 → mean replication r ≈ 0.34; per-study data from OSF XPhiReplicability_CompleteData.csv |
:::

### Coverage and known gaps

The pairs plotted above and listed in the main table are the most rigorous direct replications I was able to verify from primary sources. A handful of replication targets were checked but yielded no extractable rows, and are noted here for completeness rather than hidden.

Within RP:CB, seven further original papers were checked directly against the eLife collection and the RP:CB project summary in Errington et al. 2021 *eLife* 10:e67995. Three --- Possemato et al. 2011 *Nature* (PHGDH), Prahallad et al. 2012 *Nature* (BRAF/EGFR feedback), and Metallo et al. 2011 *Nature* (reductive glutamine metabolism) --- were registered for replication but the experiments were never completed; their OSF projects contain no data. Four others --- Bhang et al. 2015 *Nat Med* (ClonTracer), Zhang et al. 2013 *Nature* (CRM1), Morrison et al. 2012 *Science* (mouse OXPHOS), and Schwitalla et al. 2013 *Cell* (NF-κB intestinal tumor initiation) --- were outside RP:CB's 2010--2012 sampling frame or were misidentified in my earlier search and have no RP:CB replication on record. Zero additional pairs were added from these seven. The total RP:CB contribution in the main table (24 pairs from 15 papers) is the complete set that could be verified from the published Replication Studies at the time of writing.

The SCORE master dataset (Tyner et al. 2026 *Nature* 652:143--150), made public through the OSF repositories g5sny and bzfgy, was pulled directly from the `analyst_data.RData` analyst file and joined on claim ID between `orig_outcomes` (825 records) and `repli_outcomes` (427 records, filtered to version-of-record). Of 309 merged version-of-record rows, 282 had non-missing values for all four required fields (original N, original partial correlation, replication N, replication partial correlation) and are included in the main table. Effect sizes were converted from SCORE's native partial-correlation metric to Cohen's *d* using the standard *d* = 2*r*/√(1−*r*²) formula. Five additional SCORE-related rows from Landy et al. 2020 *Psychological Bulletin* and Breznau et al. 2022 *PNAS* are not plotted because the original effect sizes were unpublished pilots or not scalar; they would appear only as replication-side points.

The Protzko, Krosnick, Nelson et al. "High-Replicability Research" project (16 effects × 3 stages = 48 data points from four labs) was retrieved from its OSF materials and initially considered for inclusion as a counter-pattern dataset, since its preregistered discovery--self-replication--adversarial-replication design does not produce the shrinkage pattern that the selection-filtered literature does. The project paper was published in *Nature Human Behaviour* 8:311--319 (2024) but was **retracted in September 2024** at the publisher's initiative, on the grounds that the editors no longer had confidence in the reliability of the findings and conclusions. In keeping with the rule that this note relies only on primary sources in good standing, the 48 Protzko rows are not included in the main table and not plotted. The retraction itself is a useful data point about the literature, but the specific effect-size values cannot be cited here as settled evidence.

The preregistered-hypotheses curve on the empirical density plot is drawn from two sources. The first is Scheel, Schijen & Lakens [@scheel2021], who assembled the complete corpus of 71 psychology Registered Reports published through the end of 2018 and coded whether each study's first preregistered hypothesis was supported. The OSF dataset for that paper (`positive_results_in_registered_reports_data.tsv`) contains only the binary supported/not-supported coding, so the per-hypothesis effect sizes and sample sizes had to be extracted from the individual Registered Reports themselves. Of the 71 articles, 32 reported both an exact *N* and a native-metric effect statistic (*d*, *t*, *F*, *r*, partial η², or χ²) that could be cleanly converted to Cohen's *d* without back-calculation from *df*. The other 39 were excluded because the reported test statistic could not be converted to *d* without introducing estimation error (e.g., unstandardized regression coefficients without SEs, Bayes factors only, multilevel models with unusual *df* structures).

The second source is the Psychological Science Accelerator's Rapid-Response project **PSA-CR001** (Dorison et al. 2022, *Affective Science*) [@dorison2022], a 52-country preregistered test of loss-versus-gain framing in COVID-19 health messaging. The study preregistered four primary confirmatory outcomes (behavioral intentions, two subscales of policy support, and information seeking) and posted country-level effect sizes with 95% CIs on OSF. I pooled each primary outcome across the 52 countries via sample-size-weighted average of \|*d*\|, producing four preregistered-publication rows, each with pooled *N* ≈ 15,772 (the combined sample across all countries after applying the PSA's minimum-per-country *N* ≥ 25 cutoff).

This archived 36-row seed has now been expanded in the active Plot 3 build by adding 89 Schäfer/Schwarz preregistered psychology key-effect rows. The current plotted preregistered-results file therefore contains 125 rows. The four PSA-CR001 rows still sit near the low end of the distribution (\|*d*\| between 0.02 and 0.10 at very large *N*), while the Schäfer/Schwarz rows enter with support status marked as not coded.

### Multi-lab coordinated projects: per-lab data availability

Three separate attempts were made to pull per-lab (or per-country, per-site) effect sizes from the major coordinated multi-lab projects so that we could examine within-project heterogeneity independently of the replication-pair corpus. The results of those attempts are documented here so that readers who want to go deeper on lab-to-lab variability can find the extracted data or see what remains unavailable.

**Registered Replication Reports.** The 10 major published RRRs (Alogna verbal overshadowing Study 1, Alogna Study 2, Hagger ego depletion, Wagenmakers facial feedback, Cheung commitment/forgiveness, Eerland grammatical aspect, McCarthy hostile priming, O'Donnell professor priming, Bouwmeester intuitive cooperation, Verschuere moral self) together contributed 205 verified per-lab rows after applying each project's primary-analysis inclusion rules (e.g., McCarthy's 22-lab primary analysis, O'Donnell's 23-lab main analysis). Per-lab effect sizes were converted to Cohen's \|*d*\| from each project's posted forest-plot input: 23 rows came with Cohen's *d* directly (Hagger), 53 as risk differences with 95% CIs (Alogna Study 1 and Study 2), and 129 as raw mean differences with 95% CIs (Cheung, Eerland, McCarthy, O'Donnell, Bouwmeester, Verschuere, Wagenmakers). For the non-*d* rows, the conversion used the standard rearrangement *d* = 2·\|effect\|/(SE·√*N*), where SE is derived from the reported CI width as (CI_upper − CI_lower)/(2·1.96). The per-lab dataset (205 rows) is **not plotted on the main density plot**, because the 10 RRRs are each already represented as the replication side of one of the 555 original--replication pairs. Plotting them again as individual lab contributions would double-count the same replication evidence. The 205-row lab-level CSV is available as a downloadable artifact for readers interested in within-study heterogeneity. Of the 226 expected combinations from the announced RRR protocols, 21 labs were excluded for falling under each project's own primary-analysis rules (sample-size thresholds, native-English criteria, MTurk exclusions, and ancillary-only sample memberships), matching the RRRs' published analyses.

**Psychological Science Accelerator.** An attempt to pull per-site confirmatory effect sizes across all published PSA projects returned usable data only for PSA-CR001 (Loss-Gain framing, 208 country×hypothesis cells, of which the 4 pooled rows shown above enter the main density plot). Published PSA projects where per-site files remained inaccessible through the environment's OSF interface included PSA001 (Social Faces), Many Smiles (facial-feedback, Coles et al. 2022), PSA006 (CNI model of moral dilemmas, Bago et al. 2022 --- the OSF node returns a 410 Gone error as of April 2026), and SPAM-L (semantic priming across languages). PSA002, PSA003, PSA005, PSA008, PSA009, and the JTF-funded projects are listed on the PSA website but lack published Stage 2 papers with country-level confirmatory data as of April 2026. The per-country PSA-CR001 extract (208 cells with *N* ≥ 25) is available as a downloadable artifact; the other PSA projects will require direct OSF collaboration with project leads to obtain clean per-site confirmatory files.

**Many Labs 1--5.** An attempt to extract per-lab effect sizes across the five Many Labs projects (target: \~4,000 effect×lab combinations) failed at the data-access layer: the Many Labs OSF repositories store per-lab analyses in child components with nested directories, and the OSF API does not expose them cleanly for bulk retrieval. A handful of replicate-group-level rows could be pulled from one ML2 effect (Trolley Dilemma 2) as a demonstration, but these do not correspond to individual labs and are not suitable for within-project heterogeneity analysis. The replication-pair corpus already contains the ML1--ML5 project-level findings (13 from ML1, 28 from ML2, 10 from ML3, 1 from ML4, 6 from ML5) as their primary contribution to the density plot and the arrow plot. Recovering the \~4,000 lab-level rows would require direct scripting inside the OSF environment or collaboration with the Many Labs project leads.

### Statcheck general-literature corpus (@fig-published-d-vs-n) {#statcheck-general-literature-corpus-figure-2}

The @fig-published-d-vs-n scatter is built from the Nuijten et al. 2016 export on OSF (`150211FullFile_AllStatcheckData_Automatic1Tail.csv` at osf.io/gdr4q/download) as a fallback after the full 688,112-row Hartgerink 2016 DANS dataset could not be retrieved in the extraction environment. The fallback file contains 258,105 raw APA-formatted test results from 30,717 articles across eight psychology journals, 1985--2013: JPSP (44.3% of retained tests), Developmental Psychology, PLOS, Journal of Consulting and Clinical Psychology, JEP: General, Psychological Science, Journal of Applied Psychology, and Frontiers in Psychology.

Filtering retained 163,322 individual tests from 13,842 papers. The filter stages were: drop 24,961 rows where statcheck flagged internal inconsistency (`Error` or `DecisionError`); drop 9,289 rows with both df fields blank (mostly *Z* rows); drop 46,202 *F* rows with numerator df \> 1 (omnibus tests that cannot be converted to a single *d*); drop 11,110 χ² rows with df ≠ 1 and 2,450 χ²(1) rows without an explicit *N*; drop 591 rows with non-finite converted *d*; drop 178 rows failing the quality filter (*N* \< 4 or *N* \> 10,000,000 or \|*d*\| \> 30). After filtering, the surviving test types are *F*(1, df) at 86,681 rows, two-sample *t* at 60,186 rows, *r* at 9,036 rows, and χ²(1) at 7,419 rows. *N* was recovered from df via *N* = df + 2 for two-sample *t* and *r*, *N* = df2 + 2 for *F*(1, df2), and from the explicit `N =` field for χ²(1). Cohen's *d* was computed test-by-test: *d* = 2*t*/√df for *t*; *d* = 2√(*F*/df2) for *F*(1, df2); *d* = 2*r*/√(1 − *r*²) for *r*; *d* = 2φ/√(1 − φ²) with φ = √(χ²/*N*) for χ²(1).

For the scatter, the 163,322 tests were collapsed to 13,842 paper-level rows by taking the median \|*d*\| and median *N* within each paper. Four additional rows were dropped from the plotted subset (13,838 plotted) because their collapsed median effect size was zero or otherwise invalid for log-scale display. The regression line is a log--log OLS fit across those 13,838 points: slope −0.4465, intercept 0.678, *r*² = 0.42. Translated to the figure's framing: a tenfold increase in *N* corresponds to a 64.2% reduction in reported \|*d*\|.

**Methodological caveat worth flagging.** All *t* rows in the fallback corpus were treated as two-sample by default, because the statcheck package does not distinguish one-sample/paired *t* from two-sample *t*. Psychology's top journals use heavy within-subjects designs (repeated-measures ANOVA, paired *t*-tests), for which the two-sample formula inflates *d* by a factor of roughly √2. A sensitivity check run by the extraction pipeline shows that if every *t* were instead treated as one-sample/paired, median \|*d*\| across the corpus would drop from 0.72 to 0.58. The cross-section pattern (cloud hugging the significance boundary, 87% above *p* \< 0.05, regression slope near −0.45) is robust to this assumption; the specific median is not. Readers who want the raw test-level data or the full filter-accounting log can find `statcheck_tests.csv` (163,322 rows), `statcheck_studies.csv` (13,842 rows), and `statcheck_summary.md` in the project's downloadable artifacts.

### Brodeur economics corpus (@fig-published-d-vs-n, green circles) {#brodeur-economics-corpus-figure-2-gold-squares}

110 of the green circles in @fig-published-d-vs-n come from the Brodeur, Cook & Heyes [@brodeur2020] "Methods Matter" replication package for their 2020 *AER* paper on *p*-hacking in causal-inference economics. That paper harvested 21,740 test statistics from 684 articles across the top--25 economics journals, 2015--2018, stratified by causal-inference design (RCT, RDD, DiD, IV).

Of the 21,740 test rows in the cleaned CSV (`methods_matter_clean.csv`), only 1,350 have a non-missing `sample_size` field --- the column was populated inconsistently in the original Stata file and many articles lost the *N* metadata in the conversion. Aggregating those 1,350 tests to article-level medians (analogous to the statcheck processing) yields 112 articles with both median test-level *N* and median derived *d*; two further articles had median \|*d*\| = 0 exactly (all their tests yielded round-to-zero effects) and were dropped for log-axis compatibility. The remaining 110 are green circles.

Effect sizes were derived from the de-rounded *t*-statistics via the standard formula *d* = 2*t*/√df, treating sample_size as df. This is the same conversion used for *t*-rows in the statcheck corpus. For tests reported with coefficients and standard errors instead of *t*, the *t* was recomputed as coef/se before conversion.

The 110-article sample is a *convenience subset* of the full 684--article Brodeur corpus, not a random sample. Articles whose Stata files preserved the `sample_size` column are disproportionately those where the authors reported *N* prominently in their tables, which may correlate with study design (RCTs report *N* clearly; some DiD papers collapse data at aggregated levels that complicate *N* attribution). The stratification within the 110 is: 53 DiD articles (median *d* = 0.055, median *N* = 9,474), 25 RCT articles (*d* = 0.135, *N* = 1,093), 21 IV articles (*d* = 0.123, *N* = 4,441), and 13 RDD articles (*d* = 0.112, *N* = 3,596). Until the full corpus can be recovered with *N* attached, the 110-article overlay should be read as a preliminary cross-discipline check rather than a definitive economics distribution.

The companion "Star Wars" dataset (Brodeur, Lé, Sangnier & Zylberberg 2016; 50,000 tests from AER, JPE, QJE, 2005--2011) [@brodeur2016] is available in our archive but its public CSV does not include a sample-size column, so those 17,883 test-level observations cannot currently be placed on the log-*N* vs log-*d* axes of @fig-published-d-vs-n. They would contribute a classic Brodeur *z*-curve (density of test statistics) but not a scatter. Recovering *N* from the original ICPSR Stata file (openicpsr project 113633) is pending; it would expand the economics overlay by roughly two orders of magnitude.

### Havránek EIS micro subset (@fig-published-d-vs-n, green circles) {#havranek-eis-micro-subset-figure-2-gold-squares}

41 of the green circles in @fig-published-d-vs-n come from the Havránek [@havranek2015] meta-analysis of the elasticity of intertemporal substitution, which harvested 2,735 EIS estimates from 169 studies published in economics journals through 2014. The raw meta-file mixes two incompatible study designs: macro time-series work where *N* counts quarters of aggregate consumption, and micro panel work where *N* counts individual households. Only the micro subset is comparable to psychology's statcheck corpus and to the Brodeur causal-inference corpus, where *N* counts experimental units.

The filter applied to build the @fig-published-d-vs-n overlay proceeded in three steps. First, restrict to `micro == 1` --- 41 studies using household-level panel data where *N* = individuals --- leaving 512 estimates. Second, winsorize to \|EIS\| ≤ 5; this drops 24 of 512 estimates (4.7%), all implausible-magnitude outliers for an elasticity parameter whose true value theory and micro evidence put in roughly \[0, 1\]. Third, convert each estimate to a Cohen's-*d* analogue using *d* = 2\|*t*\|/√*N* with *t* = EIS/SE, the same conversion used for *t* rows in the statcheck corpus and for test statistics in the Brodeur corpus. Estimates were then collapsed to study-level medians (median \|*d*\| at median *N* within each study), paralleling the paper-level collapse used for the blue and the other-green point clouds. This yields 41 paper-level points; three fall below the plot's *d* = 0.01 floor and are omitted from the visible scatter.

Sub-corpus profile in isolation: median *N* = 5,704 individuals, median \|*d*\| = 0.072, 66% above *p* \< 0.05, standalone log--log slope −0.55 (*r* = −0.79). The cleaned micro subset with columns `estimate_id, study_id, study, country, eis, se, sample_size, abs_t, d_proxy, is_micro, is_panel, ols, tsls, ml, bayes, pubyear` is available as `havranek_eis_comparable.csv` in the project's downloadable artifacts (486 rows, 41 studies).

### Gechert--Havránek σ meta-analysis (@fig-published-d-vs-n, green circles) {#gechert-havranek-sigma-figure-2-gold-squares}

121 of the green circles in @fig-published-d-vs-n come from the Gechert--Havránek--Iršová--Kolcunová [@gechert2022] meta-analysis of the capital-labor elasticity of substitution σ, published in the *Review of Economics and Statistics* (2022). The meta-file contains 3,186 estimates from 121 studies spanning 1961--2017, harvested from production-function and factor-demand regressions in the macroeconomics and growth literatures. Unlike the EIS dataset, the σ dataset does not separate micro from macro study designs along a single indicator; it instead carries three flags (`is_panel`, `is_crosssec`, `is_timeseries`) identifying the data structure behind each estimate. For the @fig-published-d-vs-n overlay we did not filter by design; the 121 paper-level medians include all three design types.

The filter applied was: winsorize to \|σ\| ≤ 5 (drops \~0.8% of estimates as implausible magnitudes, since σ is bounded below by 0 and above by \~2 for plausible production technologies); convert to Cohen's-*d* analogue via *d* = 2\|*t*\|/√*N* with *t* = σ/SE; collapse to study-level medians. Sub-corpus profile in isolation: median *N* = 44, median \|*d*\|  = 1.48, 87% above *p* \< 0.05, standalone log--log slope  −0.41 (*r* = −0.55). Note the very small median *N*: σ studies often estimate production functions on country--year or industry--year panels with dozens rather than thousands of observations, which places them in the same sample-size regime as psychology and at the small-*N* extreme of the econ overlay. The cleaned subset with columns `study_id, study, country, sigma, se, sample_size, abs_t, d_proxy, is_panel, is_crosssec, is_timeseries, pubyear` is available as `havranek_sigma_comparable.csv` in the project's downloadable artifacts (3,161 rows, 121 studies).

**Robustness note across the three econ sub-corpora.** The three standalone slopes are −0.45 (Brodeur), −0.41 (σ), and −0.55 (EIS). The combined slope across all 272 paper-level points is -0.55 (*r* = -0.84). The EIS slope is steeper than the pure-filter prediction of −0.5; the other two match it within noise. The EIS case is consistent with residual heterogeneity in true EIS values correlated with study design (smaller EIS studies can load onto sub-literatures with larger true effects or tighter instruments), which is additive to the filter slope rather than substitutive. Cluster-robust standard errors on `study_id` are warranted if anyone wants a confidence interval on the combined econ slope; one estimate = one observation here overweights studies that contribute many estimates, but that does not bias the slope estimate itself, only its standard error.

*The upshot of Appendix D: the ratio of original-to-replication effect sizes is not a bug at the edges of psychology. It is the central tendency of the literature.*

## Appendix E --- Material retired from the main flow (reference archive) {#appendix-e-reference-archive}

The four subsections below were previously cited by figures in the main text. The figures they document have been removed from the current flow, but the data and notes are kept here as a deliberate reference archive so the analysis is not lost. Some of it may later return as standalone figures or medicine-specific supplements, but it no longer sits in the main manuscript as an unresolved placeholder.

### Preregistered hypotheses: Scheel 2021 + PSA-CR001 corpus

These archived rows are the original 36-row preregistered seed from the Scheel / Lakens and PSA-CR001 corpora. In the active data-paper flow they are plotted as part of Plot 3, alongside the newly added Schäfer/Schwarz preregistered psychology key-effect rows. Rows 1--32 are from Scheel, Schijen & Lakens [@scheel2021]: each is one hypothesis from a published psychology Registered Report, showing what the preregistered confirmatory test returned with no replication attempt. Rows 33--36 are from PSA-CR001 (Dorison et al. 2022) [@dorison2022]: each is one of four primary preregistered hypotheses tested across 52 countries, pooled to a single effect size and sample count. Supported = the hypothesis was judged supported by the authors of the original report; the Schäfer/Schwarz extension has D/N but no support-status coding.

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 40. Preregistered hypotheses: Scheel 2021 + PSA-CR001 corpus**

| # | Study | N | \|d\| | Supported | Journal |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | Kuppuraj 2018 — Online statistical learning reliability | 42 | 1.80 | yes | Royal Society Open Science |
| 2 | Grubb 2018 — Former targets capture | 80 | 1.10 | yes | Attention Perception Psychophysics |
| 3 | Zanolie 2014 — Magnitude×side Exp3 | 20 | 1.09 | yes | Frontiers in Psychology |
| 4 | Voorspoels 2014 — Coalition encoding + cue | 463 | 0.85 | yes | Frontiers in Psychology |
| 5 | van ’t Veer 2015 — Liars liked less | 155 | 0.60 | yes | Frontiers in Psychology |
| 6 | Dutilh 2017 — WM drift rate hard | 916 | 0.58 | yes | Attention Perception Psychophysics |
| 7 | van ’t Veer 2015 — Liars trusted less | 155 | 0.56 | yes | Frontiers in Psychology |
| 8 | Dutilh 2017 — WM drift rate easy | 916 | 0.49 | yes | Attention Perception Psychophysics |
| 9 | Rotteveel 2015 — Lever congruency Exp2 | 50 | 0.49 | no | Frontiers in Psychology |
| 10 | Steegen 2014 — Guess averaging immediate | 471 | 0.40 | yes | Frontiers in Psychology |
| 11 | Rowland 2014 — RIF Exp2 | 140 | 0.38 | yes | Frontiers in Psychology |
| 12 | Ong 2015 — Comfort food MTurk | 366 | 0.38 | no | Frontiers in Psychology |
| 13 | Verkoeijen 2014 — Spacing inductive learning | 123 | 0.37 | yes | Frontiers in Psychology |
| 14 | Evers 2014 — Diagnosticity Study 1b | 199 | 0.36 | yes | Frontiers in Psychology |
| 15 | Rotteveel 2015 — Lever congruency Exp1 | 99 | 0.35 | no | Frontiers in Psychology |
| 16 | Steegen 2014 — Guess averaging delayed | 140 | 0.34 | yes | Frontiers in Psychology |
| 17 | Ong 2015 — Comfort food SG | 198 | 0.32 | no | Frontiers in Psychology |
| 18 | Rowland 2014 — RIF Exp1 | 72 | 0.31 | yes | Frontiers in Psychology |
| 19 | van ’t Veer 2015 — Skin temp observing liar | 155 | 0.31 | yes | Frontiers in Psychology |
| 20 | Kvetnaya 2018 — Irrelevant speech spatial | 40 | 0.29 | no | J European Psych Students |
| 21 | Bombari 2017 — Body posture felt power | 200 | 0.28 | yes | Comprehensive Results in Social Psychology |
| 22 | Grubb 2018 — Feedback capture diff | 80 | 0.20 | no | Attention Perception Psychophysics |
| 23 | Voorspoels 2014 — Race encoding + coalition cue | 463 | 0.18 | yes | Frontiers in Psychology |
| 24 | Zanolie 2014 — Magnitude×side Exp2 | 24 | 0.17 | no | Frontiers in Psychology |
| 25 | Jackson 2017 — Posture self-concept size | 128 | 0.15 | no | Comprehensive Results in Social Psychology |
| 26 | Bombari 2017 — Body posture gambling | 200 | 0.08 | no | Comprehensive Results in Social Psychology |
| 27 | Zanolie 2014 — Magnitude×side Exp1 | 20 | 0.08 | no | Frontiers in Psychology |
| 28 | Evers 2014 — Diagnosticity Study 1a | 253 | 0.06 | no | Frontiers in Psychology |
| 29 | Rowland 2014 — Cue-independent RIF Exp3 | 67 | 0.05 | no | Frontiers in Psychology |
| 30 | Johnson 2014 — Cleanliness moral judgment Exp1 | 208 | 0.01 | no | Social Psychology |
| 31 | Johnson 2014 — Cleanliness moral judgment Exp2 | 126 | 0.01 | no | Social Psychology |
| 32 | Ong 2015 — Comfort food NL | 176 | 0.01 | no | Frontiers in Psychology |
:::

### Added preregistered rows from PSA-CR001 (Dorison et al. 2022)

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 41. Added preregistered rows from PSA-CR001 (Dorison et al. 2022)**

| # | Primary hypothesis (preregistered) | Pooled N | Pooled \|d\| | Supported |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 33 | Behavioral intentions (loss vs gain framing) | 15,772 | 0.10 | no |
| 34 | Policy support: individual autonomy subscale | 15,772 | 0.07 | no |
| 35 | Policy support: government power subscale | 15,772 | 0.08 | no |
| 36 | Information seeking (loss vs gain framing) | 15,772 | 0.02 | no |
:::

### Cochrane CDSR medicine corpus (pending --- data extracted, separate figure to be added) {#cdsr-medicine-corpus-figure-2-red-dots}

This corpus is not currently plotted in @fig-published-d-vs-n; the extract is documented here for reference and for a separate medicine-specific figure to be added later. The data come from the van Zwet & Schwab [@vanzwet2021] working extract of the Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews (CDSR), distributed via OSF project xjv9g. The raw extract contains 411,984 trial-level treatment-effect estimates from Cochrane reviews covering RCTs and non-RCTs, across efficacy / safety / dropout / bias outcomes and multiple outcomes per study. The analytic subset used here applied six filters: (i) restrict to randomized trials only; (ii) restrict to primary efficacy outcomes; (iii) require non-null effect size, standard error, and total *N*; (iv) require \|*z*\| \< 20; (v) deduplicate to one row per (review, study) pair; (vi) apply sanity bounds on *d*-proxy and \|*t*\|. This yielded 38,029 trial-level estimates from 6,330 reviews across 19 medical specialties, collapsible to 33,391 unique (trial, year) pairs.

**Why pre-2005.** The ICMJE announced mandatory prospective trial registration as a condition of publication in major medical journals in September 2004, with enforcement taking effect for trials starting July 1, 2005. The 2007 FDAAA extended mandatory registration and reporting to US-regulated trials. Together these mandates plausibly shift the filter regime for post-2005 medical trials toward a partial pre-committed state in which at least the primary outcome and design are locked before data analysis. For a cleanly filter-exposed regime in any future @fig-published-d-vs-n inclusion, we restrict CDSR to trials with `study_year < 2005`. This yields 17,320 pre-2005 paper-level medians, with effectively zero prospective pre-registration prevalence, and therefore represents a medicine sample drawn from the same filter regime as the psychology and economics populations.

For later plotting on the same log-log axes as @fig-published-d-vs-n, trials would be converted to a Cohen's-*d* analogue via *d* = 2\|*t*\|/√*N* with *t* = effect/SE, and plotted at each trial's total sample size.

Sub-corpus profile: median *N* = 74 (1919--2004 coverage is weighted toward smaller older trials), 29% above *p* \< 0.05, log--log slope -0.42 (*r* = -0.44, n = 16,921 plotted points within the \[*N* ∈ \[10, 10⁶\], *d* ∈ \[0.01, 5\]\] plot window). The above-*p*-threshold rate is lower than the psychology statcheck corpus (87%) or the economics corpora (65--87% by sub-corpus), reflecting that Cochrane aggregates trials by topic rather than by significance. The *post*-2005 CDSR subset (not plotted) has a near-identical slope (−0.41) and above-*p* rate (32%) --- i.e., restricting to pre-2005 does not visibly change the filter signature, suggesting the 2005 ICMJE change did not substantially shift the published effect-size distribution in CDSR-harvested trials. Direct separation of registered vs. unregistered post-2005 sub-populations would require linking trial names to ClinicalTrials.gov NCT numbers, which is a custom entity-resolution task not attempted here.

The cleaned extract (all 38,029 rows, both pre- and post-2005) is available as `cdsr_medicine.csv` in the project's downloadable artifacts, with `study_year` populated for 37,882 rows to enable re-filtering.

### AACT biomedical corpus (retired from main flow, data preserved below) {#aact-biomedical-corpus-figure-3-purple-plus-signs}

The 2,720 biomedical trials previously displayed alongside the 32 preregistered psychology hypotheses are drawn from the Jian Du & Xuanyu Shi 2024 Figshare derivative of the AACT (Aggregate Analysis of ClinicalTrials.gov) database [@du2024], DOI 10.6084/m9.figshare.24225166. This is a **fallback derivative**, not a raw AACT extraction. The intended path --- querying the AACT monthly snapshot directly through the `studies`, `outcomes`, `outcome_measurements`, `outcome_counts`, and `result_groups` tables --- could not be executed in the build environment. A downstream rebuild against the official AACT schema or the DuckDB mirror is available as `aact_build.py` in the project artifacts.

The derivative contains 119,968 raw pairwise-comparison rows from 8,665 unique trials. Filtering retained 23,252 primary-outcome rows (dropping secondary and exploratory outcomes), then 8,113 rows with either a CI or an explicit *p*-value from which a test statistic could be derived. Invalid-*N* and invalid-\|*d*\| rows (13 total) and rows missing a completion-year proxy (187 rows) were dropped. The remaining 8,309 rows were collapsed to 3,735 trial-outcome-level rows and then to 2,731 trial-level rows by taking the median \|*d*\| and median *N* within each trial; 17 trials fell outside the *N* ≥ 10 plot window, leaving 2,720 plotted. The phase distribution (Phase 1 *n* = 66 with median *d* = 0.69, Phase 3 *n* = 1,291 with median *d* = 0.31) is sensible and tracks the expected Phase 1 small-*N*/large-*d* to Phase 3 large-*N*/moderate-*d* gradient.

**Methodological caveats worth flagging.** The derivative does not contain arm-level means or pooled SDs, so Cohen's *d* is approximated, not computed from arm statistics. The fallback path converted each row's reported 95% CI or *p*-value into a test statistic, then used that test statistic with total enrollment under an equal-arm split assumption to derive \|*d*\|. For most two-arm Phase 2--4 trials this approximation is reasonable; for crossover trials and for trials with unequal randomization it is biased in direction and magnitude. Industry-sponsored trials are 85.6% of the sample, which is not the same composition as the broader biomedical literature --- the full NIH, academic-medical-center, and investigator-initiated trial ecosystem is under-represented. Completion year is used as a proxy for start year in any year-stratified analysis because start year is not available in the derivative.

**Registered ≠ preregistered.** Every ClinicalTrials.gov entry is *registered* in the administrative ICMJE and FDA regulatory sense. That is not the same as analytic preregistration with a locked, timestamped analysis plan. Outcomes are pre-specified, but the full statistical analysis plan can evolve between registration and reporting; investigators retain flexibility over covariate choice, subgroup emphasis, and analysis population definition. The primary-endpoint-switching literature (Chan et al., and the POST-REG studies) documents meaningful rates of outcome shifts between registration and publication even in trials that made it to journal publication. Nonetheless, the forced-disclosure norm *is* a brake on the file-drawer effect, which is consistent with the 55% above *p* \< 0.05 rate seen in the AACT cloud --- substantially below psychology's 87% but still higher than a clean locked-SAP regime would predict. Readers wanting the raw rows can find `aact_trials.csv` (3,735 trial-outcome rows), `aact_studies.csv` (2,731 trial rows), `aact_summary.md`, `aact_build.py`, and `aact_exact_query.sql` in the project's downloadable artifacts.
